claims.text: 33 Thomas Street in Lower Manhattan is a 500-foot 29-story windowless brutalist skyscraper that publicly serves as an AT&T telecommunications building but, per leaked NSA documents, is in fact the NSA mass-surveillance hub codenamed Titan Point.

station

minimalis

skyline
at night it becomes an invisible shadow
now thanks to nsa documents leaked by

building wasn't an accident
it was absolutely by design
it's not unusual to keep locations
c

claims.rationale: 33 Thomas Street is real, the dimensions and AT&T ownership are documented public record, and the NSA-Titan Point identification was confirmed in The Intercept's 2016 investigation drawing on Snowden documents (with corroboration in subsequent reporting). The 'designed to withstand nuclear war' framing is consistent with the building's Cold War design brief.

claims.text: 33 Thomas Street was built between 1969 and 1974 under a Cold War initiative called Project X — designed to be a 29-floor windowless fortress with three basement levels and 1,500-person, two-week food supply, with the primary purpose of protecting AT&T's telecommunications equipment rather than humans through a nuclear blast.

desperate to protect its infrastructure

project x project x you're kidding right
no that's what it was called
they're usually more creative the goal
of project x
was to build a skyscraper in the middle
of new york city capable of withstanding
a nuclear blast
the building would be 29 floors have no
windows
three basement levels and enough food to
last
1500 people two weeks but its primary
purpose was not to protect humans
this would be a fortress designed to

claims.rationale: John Carl Warnecke was the architect; construction dates are correct; the windowless / equipment-protection design brief is on record. The 'Project X' codename is sometimes attached to the building in popular accounts though the more commonly cited origin name is for AT&T Long Lines. The 1500-people / two-week supply figure is widely repeated but not from a single canonical primary source.

claims.text: Under the Titan Point program, the NSA — through AT&T (codenamed 'Lithium' in NSA documents) — surveils communications across at least 38 countries including U.S. allies France, Germany, and Japan, and targets organizations including the United Nations, World Bank, and IMF.

surveillance sites

faxes and internet data 33 thomas street

and japan
now it's common knowledge that att is
very coope

claims.rationale: These specific target lists (UN, World Bank, IMF, 38 countries including allies) are documented in published Snowden materials reported by The Intercept, Der Spiegel, and others. The codename 'Lithium' for AT&T cooperation is also from the leaked travel guides.

claims.text: A 1975 congressional inquiry exposed that the NSA was illegally surveilling anti-Vietnam war activists, civil rights leaders including Dr. King, Muhammad Ali, and journalists at the Washington Post and New York Times — a Fourth Amendment violation done without judicial warrants.

claims.rationale: This refers to the Church Committee hearings of 1975, which are well-documented Senate investigations. NSA's Project MINARET specifically targeted Dr. King, Muhammad Ali, journalists including Tom Wicker, and others. Solid historical record.

claims.text: The post-Church-Committee Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA) was supposed to constrain the NSA to surveilling foreign nationals via warrant from the FISA Court — but in a recent annual report the NSA had only 42 specific terrorism warrants and yet collected 151 million American phone-call records.

claims.rationale: The 42 warrants / 151 million phone-call records figures are from the ODNI Statistical Transparency Report (2017 figures published 2018). FISA's structure is correctly described. This is one of the most empirically grounded claims in the video.

dependencies.rationale: FISA exists because of the Church Committee revelations.

claims.text: Project BLARNEY, established in the early 1970s and still operating, leverages corporate partnerships like AT&T to give the NSA full-take collection — not just metadata but actual content of calls, emails, and online activity — across major banks, the United Nations, and 38 countries; outputs reach the President's Daily Briefing.

claims.rationale: BLARNEY is a real, declassified codename for an NSA / AT&T joint surveillance program described in published Snowden documents. Full-take vs. metadata-only distinction is correct. The 40 FISA orders, banking targets, and PDB destination are all in the published reporting.

claims.text: Project SKIDROWE allows NSA employees to search huge quantities of user data — emails, chat messages, message-board posts, texts, passwords, and browser history — captured both through 33 Thomas Street's landline taps and via its FCC satellite license.

claims.rationale: SKIDROWE is a real NSA codename in the Snowden corpus, used as a search/processing layer over BLARNEY-collected data. The 'browser history' framing is closer to XKEYSCORE-style retrospective search than to SKIDROWE specifically, but the broader claim about NSA full-take search capabilities is accurate.

claims.text: AT&T is one of at least 80 corporate partners the NSA has, and is documented as exceptionally cooperative — internal NSA documents describe the company as 'highly collaborative' with 'extreme willingness to help' — and the corporate-immunity bill that George W. Bush signed (and that Senator Obama voted for in 2008) effectively killed the consumer class-action lawsuits against the program.

claims.rationale: The 'highly collaborative' / 'extreme willingness to help' phrasing is a direct quote from internal NSA documents published by The New York Times in 2015. The whistleblower (Mark Klein) and Hepting v. AT&T / Jewel v. NSA litigation arc, plus the 2008 FISA Amendments Act retroactive immunity provision, are well documented. Senator Obama did vote for the 2008 FISA AA.

claims.text: AT&T has installed NSA surveillance equipment in at least 59 U.S. locations and NSA operatives are instructed to blend in as ordinary AT&T workers — meaning most AT&T employees were unaware of the in-house NSA presence until the Snowden leaks revealed it.

claims.rationale: The 59-locations figure and the NSA's 'blend in as AT&T workers' protocol are from the New York Times / ProPublica 2015 reporting on internal NSA documents. Solid sourcing.

claims.text: Americans have been complicit in their own surveillance by passively agreeing to lengthy, unread terms-of-service contracts with companies like AT&T, Google, and Facebook — surrendering Fourth Amendment-style protections in exchange for online convenience, and edging closer to a state where free speech and freedom from illegal search no longer meaningfully exist.

evidence.quote:
nterpret and even break laws to suit
their purposes
all in the name of national security now
to paraphrase ben franklin
when you trade liberty for security you
end up with neither
and there are many other companies like
att google facebook
that cooperate with the government with
such a lack of friction
that any american citizen should be
concerned those long agreements that you
sign online without reading them
every once in a while read one take note
of how many of your rights you're
surrendering
for the convenience of being able to
post pictures of your cat or leave a

the freedom from illegal searches and
spying
like the freedom to criticize the
government the freedom to publicly
disagree with popular ideas

claims.rationale: The factual core is solid — TOS agreements do erode privacy expectations and corporate-government data-sharing is real. The slippery-slope ending ('once freedoms are fully eroded, that's called slavery') is editorializing that overreaches. The Franklin paraphrase is also itself often misattributed in spirit — his original 'essential liberty / a little temporary safety' was about taxation, not surveillance.
